data.title: Tougher English rules daunting for many Hong Kong BN(O) holders: veteran teacher
data.description:
The tighter English requirements for migrants moving to the United Kingdom can be challenging for many Hongkongers applying for a bespoke visa pathway, and even for some British residents, according to a veteran teacher and a Briton who led a support group for the city’s immigrants.
The British Home Office confirmed on Friday that Hongkongers under the British National (Overseas) visa scheme must meet a higher English language level set for all migrants to the country as part of a shake-up of its immigration system.
Under the overhaul announced on Thursday, migrants to the UK will need to meet the B2 English proficiency level of the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages, up from the current B1 level.
Veteran English teacher Pauline Chow Lo-sai said that B2-level English, which requires understanding complex passages and technical discussions, speaking spontaneously with a native speaker, and a solid grounding in grammar and broad vocabulary, could be daunting for many Hongkongers.
“Do you find that many people in Hong Kong can speak spontaneously and can engage in a discussion in English? I don’t really think so,” she said.
“It would be very challenging. You know, people in Hong Kong do not actually have to use English, even for professionals.”
B1-level, in contrast, has room for more mistakes, according to Chow.
“As long as you are being understood, you can be accepted as a B1. And your vocabulary might be very limited,” she said.
Chow added that even university graduates might find B2-level English demanding.
“[If] in your profession or discipline of study, you are not always using English, and you are not required to express yourself verbally, that can still be very difficult,” she said.
“Because being able to speak and use that language is much more demanding than writing or reading.”
When asked whether one can achieve B2-level English in a short period of time, Chow stressed that the key was to engage in conversation rather than merely reading more.
“Reading, watching television, and so on, you are receiving English, you’re not producing. Speaking is another thing, you have to process what the person is speaking about, and then you have to prepare your answer,” she said.
Krish Kandiah, chairman of the Welcoming Committee for Hongkongers, a non-profit group, called the stricter English requirement controversial, as some locals might also struggle to meet it.
“B2-level is equivalent to A-level English; most British do not have A-level English, so many British people who are already residents here might well fail that standard. Again, it is proving controversial over here,” Kandiah said.
According to the concordances set by the International English Language Testing System (IELTS), B2-level English is equivalent to a score of 5.5 to 6.5 in IELTS. This score is roughly equivalent to level three in the Diploma of Secondary Education (DSE) exam, based on benchmarking studies by the Hong Kong Examinations and Assessment Authority.
In the 2025 DSE exam, 55.1 per cent of students attained level three or above in English.
Under the overhaul of the UK immigration system announced on Thursday, the five-year route to settlement for the BN(O) pathway remained unchanged, but applicants may face a proposed annual earnings threshold of £12,570 (US$16,430), or about HK$128,000, for three to five years to qualify for staying in the country.
The income threshold and other proposed changes will undergo a 12-week consultation, with changes expected from April next year.
But the stricter English language requirement is among mandatory stipulations that were not up for consultation. It remained unclear if people who are aged 65 and above would still enjoy exemption from the test.
Ying Perrett, a Liberal Democrat member of Surrey Heath Borough Council who moved to the UK via the BN(O) pathway in 2021, told the Post that she hoped the UK government would keep to its promise when it created the BN(O) route.
“As the BN(O) visa is a unique one, we hope the terms set out will not change, at least for those of us who have already moved to the UK, lock, stock and barrel,” she said.
Britain launched the BN(O) migration pathway in January 2021 in response to the Beijing-imposed national security law, approving nearly 225,000 such visas for Hongkongers and recording more than 160,000 arrivals as of September this year.
The UK government forecast that about 195,000 BN(O) visa holders would settle in the country in the next five years.
The scheme is among a number of settlement programmes covered in an overhaul of the UK’s immigration system, with the British government saying it was prioritising those contributing to the country’s economy and playing by the rules.
The Home Office said that because of record high levels of immigration under the previous government, 1.6 million migrants were set to become eligible for settlement by 2030.

data.title: Container ship fire at Los Angeles port triggers hazardous material alerts
data.description:
All crew members have been evacuated from the container ship One Henry Hudson after a fire and explosion disrupted the vessel’s power and crane operations at San Pedro port on Friday, the Los Angeles Fire Department (LAFD) said.
Hazardous materials have been identified in several bays at the port, and firefighters are wearing protective gear and using self-contained breathing equipment.
Marine units continue cooling the ship’s exterior, with no personnel allowed below deck, the LAFD said. TV footage from an ABC affiliate shows firefighting boats shooting streams of water on the ship’s sides and over containers on fire on the deck.
Four of the port’s seven container terminals have suspended operations and a waterside safety zone has been established around the vessel, port officials said.
LAFD said it has issued a shelter-in-place order for San Pedro and Wilmington due to a hazardous materials incident resulting from the container ship fire.

Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass said residents in areas under the order should remain at home with their windows closed and air conditioning systems turned off.
The One Henry Hudson sails under a Panama flag and arrived in Los Angeles on Wednesday from Tokyo’s Shinagawa port, according to LSEG data.
California Governor Gavin Newsom’s office in a social media post said that it is coordinating with local authorities to support first responders.
Mayor Bass said more than 100 firefighters are working to suppress the fire and air quality is being monitored.
The vessel is berthed at the Yusen Container Terminal, the Port of Los Angeles said in a statement.
No injuries have been reported, and fire suppression efforts are ongoing.

data.title: Why males take more risks and how to promote healthy risk-taking in teens
data.description:
On a summer afternoon nine years ago, Mark Raymond Jr dove into Lake Pontchartrain in the US state of Louisiana.
He had not realised how shallow it was – and felt his forehead hit the lake bottom. Three weeks later, he woke up from a medically induced coma, unable to use his legs or hands.
Diving accidents are life-shattering – but not uncommon. They disproportionately affect young men: up to 97 per cent of diving-induced spinal cord injuries occur in males, and most are aged 15 to 29 years.
“Anybody’s life can change in a split second like mine did,” says Raymond, who went on to found the Split Second Foundation in New Orleans and is its chief executive.

He was fortunate that his friends that day performed CPR and saved his life. He was in his mid-twenties at the time. Since then, he has become an energetic campaigner for spinal cord injury and, in particular, the dangers of diving into shallow water.
“I wasn’t paying attention to the tide. What I thought was deeper water was not. [I] dove off the boat and hit my head on the sandy bottom, and I shattered the fifth vertebra in my neck, resulting in paralysis,” he writes on his website.
“The message that I try to get across now to people, in general, is be mindful of the risk you take.”
Men generally exhibit riskier behaviour than women, which explains why accidental death is one of the most common ways men, especially younger men, die.
Accidental and often avoidable deaths – in car accidents, drownings, poisonings and falls – rank third, after heart disease and cancer, according to the US Centres for Disease Control and Prevention.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Mark Raymond Jr⚜️ (@markraymondjr)



Part of young men’s risk-taking is down to biology.
Hong Kong-based clinical psychologist Dr Quratulain Zaidi explains that, during puberty, testosterone increases significantly, which heightens competitiveness and the urge to seek sensation.
At the same time, she says, the part of the brain that helps with planning and self-control – the prefrontal cortex – is still developing and continues to do so until the mid-twenties in males. The ability to make fully formed decisions is therefore diminished.
This combination can make boys more impulsive – less likely to look before they leap – and more likely to take risks for social approval or excitement.
But there are also other reasons, which are less physiological.

Jackson Katz, author of Every Man: Why Violence Against Women is a Men’s Issue and How You Can Make a Difference, says that, while neurochemical and brain development factors partially account for the risk-taking behaviour of some young men, he “would caution against what sociologists call the ‘biologisation’ of everything”.
Katz is more interested in the cultural factors – especially gender norms – that influence the behaviour of boys and young men.
“The idea that a ‘real man’ takes risks, pushes boundaries, is fearless in the face of danger,” is not dictated by biology or genetics, he says.
“But they are gender norms that are often encouraged and sometimes required for young men to ‘man up’ or ‘prove’ their manhood.
“A healthy society would encourage young people to take some risks and test limits, but not to the extent of glorifying reckless behaviour or seeing it as a sign of strength.”

Zaidi agrees that society does not make it easy for boys and young men.
From a young age, boys are told to hide their emotions, that “big boys don’t cry”.
She has lost count of the number of times she has heard people use phrases like this with boys, “as if they have to prove their worth by being tough, hiding their tears”.
These messages, she says, can inadvertently direct them towards unhealthy, even dangerous behaviours, “teaching them that bravery means recklessness and strength means ignoring fear”.
Another theory suggests that male risk-taking is a part of evolution – that males need to take risks to show status. Females, on the other hand, adopt risk-averse strategies to protect their survival and that of their offspring.
A study in Australia found that young men are less likely to take risks when nobody is watching them, which suggests their attitude to risk is influenced by who they are with. Risk-taking, then, could be a public display of strength, sending signals to peers and potential mates.
“Many adolescents, especially boys, tend to underestimate risk and overestimate reward. They crave excitement and social acceptance, often acting before they think,” Zaidi says. “This isn’t defiance; it’s development.”
Because boys – and men – are encouraged by society to be competitive, that can also lead them to do things they have not thought hard enough about. This in turn can result in accidents, Hong Kong counsellor Michael Beckham says, “as men may feel the need to ‘prove’ themselves as men”.

Because they are generally discouraged from expressing vulnerability or anxiety, men can adopt less healthy coping strategies, which might involve risky behaviour – substance abuse, say, which can create a vicious cycle.
Finally, research suggests women are both more “loss-averse” and much less optimistic than men. The combination makes them much more risk-averse as well. The opposite is true for men – being more optimistic and less averse to loss makes them more willing to take risks.
Not all risk is bad, though. Zaidi notes an important difference between healthy and unhealthy risk-taking.
She says that the teenage years are a vulnerable time, but that it is also “a time filled with curiosity, exploration and change. Learning how to take risks in healthy ways is crucial at this stage because it helps build confidence, resilience and emotional maturity.”
When managed well, risk-taking can be a powerful force for growth. Healthy risks are those that help children stretch further than they thought they could – in speaking in public, standing up for a classmate or exploring a new skill.
These kinds of experiences encourage problem-solving, confidence and perseverance. This is what helps boys become men.

Zaidi’s tips for developing healthy risk-taking in your teen
Boys need to feel safe to take healthy risks
At home, relationships grounded in respect and open communication encourage safe exploration. At school, feeling valued gives boys the confidence to take intellectual and emotional risks. Among peers, healthy friendships can reduce or amplify risk-taking, depending on group values.
Do not eliminate risk
Guide risk-taking with care, patience and perspective. Boys need opportunities to experience challenge, uncertainty and failure – the very things that teach resilience, creativity and decision-making.
Do not be overprotective
When parents, teachers and mentors offer structure without smothering and freedom without neglect, boys develop insight along with confidence.

Understand boys’ biology
This can help parents respond with guidance rather than punishment. Instead of shutting down risk, direct it towards safe, constructive but challenging outlets such as sport, adventure and creative projects – rather than leaving them to express it through danger or defiance.
Encourage mistakes
Healthy risk-taking begins with creating a home where mistakes are part of learning. Children need to be inoculated with failure, and they must know that it is safe to fail – that missteps are not evidence of weakness but stepping stones to growth.
Parents can normalise this by sharing their own failures, talking about what they learned and focusing on the effort, not just the outcome.
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Ex-Brazil president Jair Bolsonaro arrested before 27-year sentence
data.description:
Brazil’s federal police arrested former President Jair Bolsonaro pre-emptively on Saturday, days before he was set to begin his 27-year prison sentence for leading a coup attempt, officials said.
A close aide said the embattled former leader was taken to the police force headquarters in the capital, Brasilia, from his house arrest.
The force said in a short statement, which did not name Bolsonaro, that it acted on the request of Brazil’s Supreme Court.
Neither Brazil’s federal police nor the Supreme Court provided more details.
Bolsonaro’s aide Andriely Cirino confirmed to Associated Press that the arrest took place around 6am on Saturday.
The 70-year-old former president was taken from his house in a gated community in the upscale Jardim Botanico neighborhood to the federal police headquarters, Cirino said.
Bolsonaro was placed under house arrest in early August, weeks before he was convicted in his coup trial. His lawyers were pleading with Brazil’s Supreme Court to keep him at home to serve his sentence, citing his poor health.
Like most of his colleagues, Supreme Court Justice Alexandre de Moraes, who oversaw the coup case, rarely carries out decisions on Saturdays, unless there are security risks involved.

Local media reported that Bolsonaro, who was Brazil’s president from 2019 to 2022, was expected to begin serving his sentence sometime next week after the far-right leader exhausted all appeals of his conviction for leading a coup attempt.
Saturday’s pre-emptive arrest does not mean Bolsonaro will remain at the federal police headquarters to serve his sentence. Brazilian law requires that all convicts start their sentences in prison.
One of the former president’s sons, Senator Flavio Bolsonaro, has been egging on supporters to take to the streets in defence of his father since Thursday.
Some of Bolsonaro’s supporters, who claim he is being politically persecuted, are expected to rally outside the federal police headquarters throughout the weekend.
The former president and several of his allies were convicted by a panel of Supreme Court justices for attempting to overthrow Brazil’s democracy following his 2022 election loss to President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva. Prosecutors said the coup plot included plans to kill Lula and to encourage an insurrection in early 2023.
Bolsonaro was also found guilty on charges of leading an armed criminal organisation and attempting the violent abolition of the democratic rule of law. Bolsonaro has denied wrongdoing.
He remains a key figure in Brazilian politics, despite being ineligible to run again at least until 2030, after a separate ruling by Brazil’s top electoral court. Polls show he would be a strong candidate in next year’s vote if allowed to run.
Bolsonaro is an ally of US President Donald Trump, who has called his trial a “witch hunt”. Bolsonaro was mentioned in a July order by the US administration raising tariffs on several Brazilian exports by 50 per cent. Trump on Friday dropped most of the higher tariffs on Brazilian exports.

data.title: Hong Kong Medical Council overturns decision to end inquiry into alleged blunder
data.description:
Hong Kong’s embattled Medical Council has overturned its earlier decision to terminate an inquiry, delayed for 15 years, into a paediatrician accused of a blunder that left a mainland Chinese boy permanently disabled.
The council decided on Saturday to revoke its previous ruling made on October 28 to permanently stay the proceedings of the disciplinary inquiry into Dr Sit Sou-chi following a 4½-hour review hearing.
Council chairwoman Dr Grace Tang Wai-king announced the decision at the end of the review.
The hearing was held after the council announced it would review its panel’s decision last month to terminate the inquiry into the paediatrician accused of the medical blunder 16 years ago that left the child with cerebral palsy and quadriplegia.
The panel’s decision to stay proceedings because of an eight-and-a-half-year procedural delay by its secretariat had been condemned by the child’s parents.
Mainland Chinese couple Li Zhijian and Peng Hongying lodged a complaint against Sit with the council in 2010. The doctor was accused of failing to carry out necessary and immediate examinations or provide treatment – a delay of 3½ hours – following the couple’s newborn son’s epileptic seizure on December 22, 2009.

The seizure later resulted in the baby, Li Yuanjian, developing cerebral palsy and quadriplegia, leaving him unable to care for himself for life. The boy was born at Baptist Hospital in Kowloon Tong on December 19, 2009.
The inquiry was initially scheduled for 2016 but was postponed at Sit’s request to review new evidence. It was later rescheduled for last month.
The parents told the Post earlier that they had to travel to Hong Kong and across mainland China – including to Beijing, Tianjin, Liaoning and Guangzhou – seeking treatment for their son over several years.
Secretary for Health Lo Chung-mau said the case had prompted them to reflect on the system, its procedures and whether revisions to the Medical Registration Ordinance were needed, as the Medical Council was governed by it.
Lo proposed an overhaul of the medical watchdog to enhance its efficiency, saying the government would examine the council’s composition, legal functions and procedures to improve the statutory body’s operations under potential legal amendments.
Statistics showed that more than 60 per cent of patient complaints to the council took over two years to resolve, with 162 cases exceeding four years.

data.title: China’s People’s Daily goes into anti-Takaichi overdrive, warning of Japan’s ‘demise’
data.description:
People’s Daily, the Communist Party’s flagship mouthpiece, has published yet another sharply worded attack on the Japanese leader’s comments on Taiwan, warning that Sanae Takaichi was leading her country to its “demise”.
The commentary on Saturday – written under the “Zhong Sheng” pen name often used to voice Beijing’s position on global affairs – is the fourth signed commentary on the issue in the newspaper in nine days, an unusually high rate that reflects the weight of Beijing’s criticism of Tokyo.
The Saturday commentary said the Japanese prime minister’s comments on Taiwan were motivated by “selfish political ambitions” and disregarded world peace and stability.
“Sanae Takaichi’s erroneous words and actions are leading Japan into a dangerous situation where warmongering will inevitably lead to its demise,” it added.
The People’s Daily barrage was a response to Takaichi’s comments to the Japanese parliament earlier this month that the use of force against Taiwan could be seen as a “survival-threatening situation”, a scenario that would allow Tokyo to deploy its military forces.
It was the first time a sitting Japanese prime minister had made such remarks and a departure from Japan’s long-held strategic ambiguity.
Beijing saw the remarks as provocative and interference in China’s internal affairs, and it demanded that the Japanese leader retract her statement. Chinese officials have also framed the comments as Tokyo leaning into its pre-war path of militarism.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.


The “Zhong Sheng” piece on Saturday repeated Beijing’s position that Takaichi’s comments constituted a “blatant interference in China’s internal affairs, a serious departure from international law … and a disruption of regional and even global peace and stability”.
It said the Japanese leader openly issued a threat of force against Beijing with her remarks.
“This not only shows a complete lack of respect for history but also represents a blatant challenge to peace. Such politicians, who disregard the future of the nation and world peace and stability for their own political gain, will ultimately only harm themselves, their country, and the world,” it warned, referring to Japan as a “defeated nation” of World War II.
The commentary suggested that Takaichi’s remarks on Taiwan were politically driven, saying the Japanese leader was seeking to consolidate support by “hyping up the ‘China threat theory’”.
“Such a practice of tying the country’s future and destiny to personal political ambition will only lead Japan astray and leave it badly bruised,” it said.
“Sanae Takaichi’s erroneous words and actions have poisoned Sino-Japanese relations and are forcing the Japanese people to pay the price for their political ambitions.”
Noting that China was Japan’s largest trading partner, it warned that “if Japan refuses to repent or even continues its mistakes, China will have no choice but to take more severe and resolute countermeasures”.
Already this month, China has issued a travel advisory urging its citizens to avoid travelling to Japan. While Beijing cited safety concerns for the advisory, it is largely viewed as a retaliatory response to Takaichi’s comments.
The harsh tone and wording of Saturday’s People’s Daily commentary was similar to others by the newspaper on the same issue. One – published last Friday – suggested that Takaichi was trying to link Taiwan to Japan’s security interests, saying it was “an excuse for Japan’s military expansion, and harboured dangerous signs of a resurgence of militarism”.
“The Japanese side must immediately correct its provocative and transgressive words and actions, retract its egregious statements, and stop its dangerous and reckless actions in the military and security fields. Otherwise, Japan must bear all the consequences,” it said.
Another commentary on Wednesday also warned: “By defying the strong opposition of the international community and insisting on hitching itself to the chariot of splitting China, Japan will only end up suffering the consequences of its own actions.”
On Saturday, Chinese media also quoted airline schedule tracking platform AeroRoutes as saying that nine major Chinese airlines had started cutting back on flights from China to Japanese cities.
This includes state-run national carrier Air China, which will reduce flights between Shanghai and Osaka from 21 to 16 round trips starting November 30, according to the report. Flights between Chongqing and Tokyo will be reduced from seven to four.
Chinese airlines started offering full refunds for flights to Japan last week, with reports suggesting that close to 500,000 tickets to Japan have been cancelled.

data.title: China ‘wheelchair anchor’ Liang Yi becomes first disabled TV host after spinal cord injury
data.description:
Chinese television personality Liang Yi became China’s first “wheelchair anchor” despite a sudden illness taking away her health and bright future.
Born in central China’s Hunan province in 1979, in 2001 Liang became an anchor at Hunan TV, one of China’s most popular television channels for the following two decades.

She had a bright future, but just four months after starting the job, she suddenly lost consciousness while backstage preparing for a show.
Liang was diagnosed with a spinal cord vascular malformation, which ruptured and compressed her central nervous system and paralysed her.
The rare condition is believed to be congenital.
The 22-year-old Liang recovered feeling in her upper limbs following surgery, but was paralysed from her chest down.

She said she initially felt anger and despair as she languished in hospital.
Liang’s feelings changed after she experienced strangers treating her kindly and becoming a Good Samaritan, cheering fellow patients up in hospital.
She also met renowned Chinese writer Shi Tiesheng, who was paralysed from the waist down at 21, but later moved many spirits with his optimistic and insightful writing.
Shi told her: “We are unaware of the despair we will encounter when we are young. The only thing we can do is read and think, so that we can fight the despair when we encounter it.”

Liang studied hard for three months and became a master’s student in broadcasting and hosting at China’s prestigious Communication University in 2011.
In 2012, she made a return to centre stage and became China’s first “wheelchair host” at Hunan TV.
Over the past decade, Liang hosted television and radio programmes, published books and attended activities to encourage and promote other people with disabilities.
Liang maintained a professional attitude to work despite her physical limitations.

She leaves home three hours in advance to prepare for recordings, even though her home is just 20 minutes away from work.
She also uses a cushion to keep her back straight during recordings. This makes her voice unstable, so she practices using the cushion at home to get used to it.
Liang said she is grateful to her mother, who is 11cm shorter than her but carried her to her wheelchair multiple times a day and accompanied her to work.
Liang said she made it to where she is today thanks to perseverance.
“Do not underestimate the power of persistence. We overcome difficulties and challenges in our life and become better people because of it,” she said.

data.title: China’s military eases physical requirements for pilots based on new cockpits
data.description:
China’s military has eased stringent physical requirements for pilots, based on the improved cockpits of the People’s Liberation Army’s newest warplanes.
Changes to height, weight and limb length requirements are among several revisions made to the physical selection criteria for pilots, military mouthpiece PLA Daily reported on Monday.
The report did not include the full revised set of criteria but said the changes had taken into account factors such as aircraft upgrades, advances in medical technology, and physical and mental changes in the population.
“Given changes to the position of the control stick and the increased adjustable angle of the seat in new fighter jets, the requirements for arm length and lower limb length have been appropriately lowered,” the report said.
China’s fifth-generation fighter jets including the J-20 and the J-35 have a sidestick configuration, as used by the F-16, F-35s and Dassault Rafale.


That configuration puts the control stick to the side – rather than at the centre – of the cockpit to improve ergonomics, since pilots no longer need to extend their arms to steer the centre yoke.
It also requires less physical effort – especially in high G-force situations – to steer the aircraft with a sidestick.
The sidestick layout is mostly used by modern fly-by-wire aircraft that use electronic signals rather than manual systems, eliminating the need for a large control column. Sidesticks also offer space efficiency and give the pilot a clearer view of the instrument panel.
Most of China’s new generation of fighter jets are equipped with this new layout.
Fourth-generation ejection seats for PLA warplanes have also been introduced that can be adjusted to suit pilots of various heights and weights, according to a report on state broadcaster CCTV in 2022. It said the system could also adjust the ejection force according to the pilot’s weight.
According to PLA Daily, the revised standards for pilots include the use of a 24-hour heart monitoring device, on top of routine electrocardiograms. It said ECGs were seen as “insufficient for a comprehensive assessment” of certain conditions.
One doctor who has screened pilot candidates for the Chinese military said testing was rigorous.
She said the most important tests were eyesight, the Bárány chair that tests balance and spatial orientation, and multitasking, where the candidate must operate a simple flight simulator while doing mathematical calculations.
“It’s really a pity seeing candidates passing these major hurdles but then failing to meet the stringent requirements for height, weight and limbs,” said the doctor, who spoke on condition of anonymity.
“Relaxing these requirements will definitely allow more candidates to join the ranks of pilots without compromising their combat performance,” she added.

The United States Air Force has struggled in recent years to recruit and retain pilots and has introduced policies including higher retention bonuses.
Meanwhile, China’s military has widened pilot recruitment in the past few years to meet the needs of its rapidly expanding aircraft carrier fleet and air force.
The PLA Navy loosened the selection criteria for pilots for its carrier-based aircraft and now allows candidates who have had vision correction surgery to apply.
In addition, the age limit for navy pilot cadets has been extended from 24 to 26 so that postgraduates can apply.

data.title: Hong Kong student delegation opts out of Japan exchange programme amid tensions
data.description:
A Hong Kong student delegation has decided to pull out of a regional exchange programme organised by Japan next month amid a recent diplomatic dispute between Beijing and Tokyo.
The Education Bureau confirmed on Saturday that Hong Kong would withdraw from this year’s Japan-East Asia Network of Exchange for Students and Youths Programme, citing safety concerns.
“In light of the recent increase in attacks on Chinese citizens in Japan and to ensure the safety of teachers and students, the Hong Kong delegation has, after careful consideration, withdrawn from this year’s [exchange programme],” the bureau said in a statement.
According to media reports, some 18 students and teachers from Hong Kong were supposed to fly to Japan for this year’s event, scheduled to start on December 7.
The Japanese government announced the programme in 2007 to promote people-to-people exchanges between Japan and various countries in the Asia-Pacific region, with the aim of establishing solidarity in Asia through youth exchange.
Hong Kong has been invited to join the programme since 2008.
A spokesman for Japan’s consulate in Hong Kong could not be reached for comment on Saturday afternoon.
The bureau’s decision also coincided with an escalating diplomatic spat between China and Japan over recent comments by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi on defending Taiwan.
In an apparent rebuttal by Beijing, the foreign ministry urged Chinese people to reconsider travel to Japan, citing “safety risks” amid a rise in cases of attacks against Chinese people.
The Hong Kong government also followed suit and updated its travel alert to advise residents to exercise caution and prioritise personal safety while in Japan.
Hong Kong flag carrier Cathay Pacific also said it would allow travellers holding tickets to Japan to “modify” their travel plans and promised to “do our utmost to provide flexibility and accommodate their needs”.
Cathay’s budget arm, HK Express, also made similar arrangements.
Greater Bay Airlines revealed it would offer refunds for Japan-bound flights, applicable to tickets bought on or before November 15 and scheduled to fly on or before December 31.
Hong Kong Airlines said on Friday that it was committed to helping affected passengers with their travel arrangements. Passengers who booked their flights to Japan directly can contact its customer service team or travel agent for help.
About 491,000 air tickets from mainland China to Japan were cancelled within a short period after carriers swiftly announced refunds or itinerary changes for flights booked through December 31, according to a veteran aviation analyst.
Japan’s foreign affairs ministry, however, rejected that there had been a surge in criminal cases against Chinese nationals in the country, saying “such an assertion is incorrect”.
It cited national police agency figures that the number of murder, robbery and arson cases involving Chinese people as victims in Japan had dropped from 48 in 2023 to 45 in 2024.
As of end-October this year, there were 28 such cases, a drop from 35 for the same period in 2024.

data.title: Interactive museum dedicated to the Shroud of Turin opens in Southern California
data.description:
An interactive museum dedicated to the Shroud of Turin, which some Christians claim was Jesus’ burial cloth, opens its doors to the public this week at the Christ Cathedral campus in Southern California.
“The Shroud of Turin: An Immersive Experience”, a US$5 million exhibition in Garden Grove featuring 360-degree projection rooms, Shroud of Turin replicas, interactive kiosks, and a life-size sculpture of Christ, was conceived over three years and funded through private donations.
The content was created primarily by Othonia, a Rome-based group dedicated to the examination of the shroud. The original – a cloth 4.4 metres (14 feet 5 inches) long and 1.1 metres wide – is kept in a bulletproof, climate-controlled case housed in the Cathedral of Saint John the Baptist in Turin, Italy.
The California museum – covering 930 square metres (10,000 sq ft) – contains a life-size laminated visual of the shroud stretched over a wall. It shows the faint image of a man with wounds similar to Christ’s in the story.
The Vatican, while making no claim to the original’s authenticity, has called the cloth a powerful symbol of Christ’s suffering. In 1988, carbon-dating of scraps of the cloth put it as being made in the 13th or 14th century, but some say the results might have been skewed by contamination, calling for larger samples to be analysed.
Many insist that the cloth contains pollen from Jerusalem and is woven in a pattern that was unique to the first century.

The cloth, considered a relic by many Christians and which may never leave Turin, was most recently on display in the spring of 2015.
The new exhibition will remain at least until 2030 on the Christ Cathedral campus, next to the glass tower and sanctuary erected by televangelist Robert H Schuller that is now home to the Catholic Diocese of Orange.
Timothy Freyer, an auxiliary bishop at the diocese, has high hopes that the exhibition will reinforce for visitors the power of God’s love for all.
“My hope is that people of faith will have stronger faith, people who are doubting will come to belief, and people with no faith will begin the questioning process and then come to faith,” he said.
One of the highlights of the immersive experience is a recreation of the story of Christ’s resurrection on Easter morning, with museum visitors sitting in the tomb and watching the shrouded body disappear in a flash of light. The exhibition also features replicas of the spear believed to have pierced Christ’s chest and the helmet of thorns placed on his head.
Philip Rizzo, a parishioner at St Bonaventure Catholic Church in Huntington Beach who was part of a group that got a preview, said seeing these items up close created a powerful visual representation of Christ’s suffering and sacrifice.
“We’re tangible beings, not just spirit and light,” Rizzo said. “Science really helps make that connection.”
While some scientists have criticised the eagerness of Christians to authenticate the cloth, the new exhibition had its origins in such curiosity. Its main proponent was August Accetta, a gynaecologist who opened up the Shroud Centre of Southern California in 1998 and has studied the cloth for over three decades.
Accetta, who grew up a Catholic, said it was the Shroud of Turin that converted him from agnostic back to Catholicism. His fascination continued after meeting John Jackson, a nuclear physicist, who in 1978 led a 40-person team of scientists as part of the Shroud of Turin Research Project. Their study determined that the cloth was not a manufactured work of art, but contained human bloodstains and that no known physical, chemical or biological process could adequately explain how the image on the Shroud was formed.
Accetta believes the image was created by a burst of radiation and that a massive amount of energy would have been required to create the image without burning the cloth.
He believes the body of Christ became light and the shroud simply collapsed, leaving the imprint of his face on the white linen.

Robert Spitzer, founder of the Magis Centre, a non-profit that uses science to defend the Catholic faith, said the cloth bears “perfect bloodstains down to the edges”, which does not happen when a shroud is simply pulled off a dead body. The stains should be smeared and fragmented if that were the case, he said. Like Accetta, Spitzer believes the etching on the cloth was likely caused by particle radiation.
Spitzer added that he does not need a relic to explain his faith because that comes from his belief in scripture and Christ’s resurrection.
“But it’s given me a little deeper insight into God and how he works,” he said.
Nora Creech, director of Othonia in the US, said the Rome-based organisation was founded by Hector Guerra, a Catholic priest who dreamed of creating 100 exhibitions globally. He built the first flagship exhibition in Jerusalem and followed with others in Rome, Poland, Mexico and the US. This latest one is the first to feature an immersive experience, which was created by a California-based studio.
Creech said they decided to begin the movie-like immersive experience with Christ’s life story from birth to crucifixion and end with the resurrection and the message that “Jesus is still with us today”.
“One of the quotes in the movie is that the cross received Jesus alive and turned him over to the shroud, dead. The shroud received Jesus dead and turned him over to us alive in the Eucharist,” she said.
Rudy Dichtl, a Catholic and one of the scientists who formed the 1978 research team, still remembers every moment spent touching and holding the cloth in his hands.
Dichtl said as a scientist he acknowledges there is limited evidence in science to conclusively say the cloth was the one that covered the body of Christ.
“But as a Christian,” he said, “I do believe it is the burial cloth of Christ.”
